Tab 01 / Current State Assessment

Chase UK is paying £1.4M/year for a CDP
and getting an event router.

This report assesses mParticle's current deployment across chase.co.uk and the Chase mobile app. All findings are based on C4 architecture diagrams, the Chase Germany Ad Tech data flow, and stakeholder interviews across Martech, Marketing, Product and Data Engineering.

29%Use Cases Working
25%Partially Working
46%Not Working
38Feature Requests
58%In Permanent Backlog
Executive Summary

✅ What's Working

  • Data Lake integration is well-architected — dual Kinesis streams provide a reliable source of truth for Analytics and Monitoring.
  • moEngage is connected bidirectionally, enabling closed-loop campaign attribution and live campaign operations.
  • AppsFlyer on-device kit handles SKAN and ATT consent flows correctly.
  • Schema enforcement via Data Master keeps event quality manageable.

🔴 What's Broken Now

  • Paid media audiences — GMP, Google Ads, Meta, Apple Search Ads, Rakuten — bypass mParticle entirely via manual file push. Slow, governance risk, blocks real-time activation.
  • AppsFlyer S2S not implemented — kit-only architecture causes data discrepancy between AppsFlyer and Amplitude. af_id is not captured as a device dimension.
  • DSR forwarding failure — CUID-only requests are not forwarded to AppsFlyer. Active GDPR compliance gap with regulatory exposure.
Critical: 11 items of technical debt are on record, 4 of them rated Critical — including no Terraform support after four years of requests, and DSR forwarding failure with direct GDPR implications. Only 2 of 38 feature requests have been fully delivered.
Detailed Configuration Assessment

mParticle ingests raw event data from Chase apps, web and partner platforms into a central backbone. The schema enforcer validates, drops and deduplicates events into a deterministic single Master Profile (mPID), allowing clean, identity-enriched data to egress to downstream destinations.

  • moEngage is bidirectional — enables closed-loop campaign attribution.
  • Data Lake source is bidirectional — enables enrichment from backend systems, full audit trail for compliance, and is the source of truth for all raw event streams, dropped/quarantined events, media attribution and engagement data.
  • No offline sources — Call centre, high-street store locations, in-person interactions are not ingested.
  • No third-party enrichment — Experian or equivalent paid data partnerships are not connected.
  • Significant SDK bloat — 5 on-device SDKs running simultaneously (mParticle, AppsFlyer, Amplitude, moEngage, Split).

mParticle currently has 5 configured destinations. The native Audience Builder and real-time audience syndication to ad platforms is not currently used for any paid media, base comms, or experimentation use cases.

mParticle operates in a zero-data-copy architecture, which is a genuine security advantage for a regulated bank — it means PII never leaves Chase's own environment. This architecture would support expanded use cases (Experian enrichment, paid partnerships) without physically copying data into mParticle's warehouse. This capability is currently dormant.

mParticle's IDSync creates and maintains a unified customer profile (mPID) by stitching together identifiers from all input sources. This is the backbone of accurate audience syndication and cross-channel personalisation.

  • mPID (anonymous cookie) is dropped by the web SDK and added to an ID sync.
  • af_id is assigned by the AppsFlyer app SDK on every new app launch and added to ID sync.
  • CUID (hashed email) is passed back to mParticle upon successful KYC completion — at this point mParticle joins CUID with af_id since KYC happens within the app.
  • Known gap: If the web → app → KYC journey is not one unbroken session, mPID may not correctly join to the profile. Web identifiers, IDFV and GAID are pending configuration that would significantly improve profile consolidation.
  • Recommended: Implement explicit logout calls between sessions to minimise collision probability.

mParticle's Data Master provides JSON schema-based Data Plans that validate event names, attribute types, required fields and allowed values before any event is forwarded downstream. This is the primary governance mechanism currently in use.

Data Plans do not perform deduplication of event streams. The Rules Engine — which enables event-level transformations (modify, enrich, filter, block) between input and output — is minimally used. At full utilisation it would be a significant capability multiplier.

Neither Smartype (compile-time SDK validation) nor Data Catalog (central event registry with ownership) are currently implemented at Chase — both are included in the existing licence.

Marketing: Significantly constrained. The two missing features are audience orchestration (with PII-enriched data) and real-time syndication to destination marketing tools. Currently, all data with PII bypasses mParticle entirely through a manual file push mechanism that requires individual security reviews for every third-party ad platform (classed as elevated risk). This blocks real-time optimisation entirely.

Product: Leverages mParticle for central governance and audit/security guardrails across the Martech stack. The mParticle wrapper kit enables deployment of one SDK on-device to control all vendor SDKs simultaneously. While logically sound, this setup has not evolved since 2020. The absence of S2S integration has created significant gaps.

Data Engineering: Uses two Kinesis streams (Analytics and Monitoring) to transmit data from mParticle to the Data Lake. This integration is working well and is the most mature part of the current deployment.

The core ingestion pipeline functions adequately under normal conditions. However, the platform lacks throughput management controls — no self-throttling, buffering, or selective replay of failed events.

GOC Feature Request Status (2020–2025): 38 feature requests submitted. 58% in permanent backlog. 7 rejected outright — including the DSR fix and identity collision diagnostics, both with explicit compliance implications. Only 2 of 38 fully delivered.

Chase is stress-testing the platform at a scale and compliance bar that exposes gaps most customers never encounter. These documented gaps are a strong instrument for contract renegotiation.

Trust: Indicators from the internal GOC Tracker show consistently depleting trust from engineering and marketing teams.

Technical Debt Register
#
Issue
Severity
Detail
1
DSR Forwarding Failure
Critical
CUID-only deletion requests are not forwarded to AppsFlyer. Root cause: AppsFlyer's OpenDSR API requires a device_id. Active GDPR compliance exposure.
2
No Terraform Support
Critical
4 years of requests outstanding. No IaC means no version-controlled configuration, no repeatable deployments, no audit trail for config changes.
3
Identity Collision Diagnostics
Critical
Request rejected (GOC 0005670). No tooling to diagnose when two distinct users incorrectly merge. Compliance implications explicitly flagged by Chase.
4
API Token Auth Not Used
Critical
Chase accesses mParticle via browser session cookies rather than persistent bearer tokens. Security posture below JPMC standards for a banking environment.
5
Egress Self-Throttle
High
Request rejected. Chase required a custom Lambda workaround. No per-destination outbound rate controls available natively.
6
Amplitude Cohort Feedback
High
Rejected (GOC 000182). Amplitude cohorts cannot natively feed mParticle audience activation. Blocks closed-loop experimentation.
7
AppsFlyer S2S Missing
High
AppsFlyer runs kit-only. af_id not captured as device dimension on mParticle. Causes data discrepancy between AppsFlyer and Amplitude attribution.
8–11
Smartype, Data Catalog, IDFV/GAID, Offline Sources
Medium
Features included in licence but not deployed. Each represents a compounding opportunity cost that grows the longer activation is deferred.

Investment note: mParticle at £1.4M is Chase UK's largest individual MarTech infrastructure investment. It manages IT & Risk compliance, operations, marketing activation, and the data pipe for all downstream platforms. Hidden operational cost — a dedicated CDP team primarily engaged in support and workaround development specific to JPMC needs — compounds this significantly.
Tab 02 / CDP Platform Comparison

mParticle vs Segment —
Verified Product Capability Review

This comparison evaluates native product capabilities, not Chase's current deployment. Where Chase's deployment is a relevant data point it is called out separately. All claims marked as unverified require direct vendor confirmation before inclusion in a formal evaluation. Prepared February 2026.

2.1 — Data Collection & Schema Governance
CapabilitymParticleSegment
Event Collection (Web & Mobile) ✓ Native — wrapper-kit SDK model ✓ Native — leaner core SDK
Schema Governance ✓ Data Plans + Data Catalog ✓ Protocols — violations dashboard
Compile-Time SDK Validation ✓ Smartype ✓ Typewriter — functionally equivalent
Nested JSON Ingestion ✗ Flat JSON only — must pre-flatten ✓ Accepts nested objects natively
Batch Buffering Control ⚠ Basic; no granular synthetic pool control ✓ Configurable per-destination batch settings
Chase context: Neither Smartype nor Data Catalog is currently deployed. The nested object constraint is a real mParticle product gap Chase encounters.
2.2 — Identity Resolution
CapabilitymParticleSegment
Matching Method ✓ Deterministic only (IDSync) ✓ Deterministic only (Unify) — no probabilistic
Identity Strategies ✓ 5 configurable strategies with priority hierarchy ⚠ Single configurable framework with ID rules
Warehouse-native Identity ✓ ComposeID (2023) — runs in warehouse Chase controls ⚠ Via SQL only — no native equivalent
Profile Diagnostics ⚠ IDSync /search API — basic profile lookup ✓ Profiles Insights — visual merge reason tooling
Merge Protection ✓ Priority/strategy rules prevent collisions ✓ Merge Protection algorithm — auto-detection
Chase context: IDSync is configured with CUID and AppsFlyer device ID only. IDFV, GAID, and mPID are not fully utilised — a deployment gap, not a product limitation. Chase's identity collision diagnostic request was rejected (GOC 0005670).
2.3 — Audience Activation
CapabilitymParticleSegment
Rule-Based Audience Builder ✓ Native + Cortex ML audience engine ✓ Segment Engage Audiences — equivalent
Paid Media Syndication ✓ Native connectors — Google Ads, Meta, ASA, Rakuten ✓ Native via Twilio Engage — equivalent
Predictive ML Audiences ✓ Cortex — churn, LTV, propensity (Enterprise licence) ✗ No native ML — requires external platform
Composable / Warehouse-Native ✓ Zero-copy — PII stays in Chase's warehouse ⚠ Reverse ETL — data moves into Segment's infra
Amplitude Cohort Feedback ✗ Not supported (GOC 000182 rejected) ✓ Native — Amplitude cohorts feed Segment audiences
Chase context: Zero audience capabilities are currently in use. All paid media runs via manual file push. Cortex is included in the current licence but has never been activated.
2.4 — Warehouse Architecture (Key for Chase)
CapabilitymParticleSegment
Warehouse Model ✓ Zero-copy — streams directly to Data Lake ⚠ Reverse ETL — pulls data into Segment's infra
PII Security Posture ✓ PII never leaves Chase's environment ⚠ Reverse ETL makes Segment a PII processor — new GDPR agreement required
ComposeID (Warehouse Identity) ✓ Identity resolution runs in Chase's warehouse ✗ No native equivalent — identity in Segment pipeline
For a regulated bank, zero-copy is a meaningful architectural advantage. Segment's Reverse ETL model introduces Segment as a processor of PII extracted from Chase's Data Lake — triggering additional GDPR obligations and security review.
2.5 — Platform Governance
CapabilitymParticleSegment
Terraform / IaC ✓ Native provider available (not deployed at Chase) ✓ Mature, widely used Terraform provider
Egress Rate Control ⚠ Limited — Chase uses custom Lambda workaround ✓ Granular per-destination throttling — native
Workspace RBAC ⚠ Partial — Production access limited beyond read ✓ Granular read/write/admin per workspace
Observability / Debugging ✓ Live Stream — real-time payload inspection ✓ Debugger + Profiles Insights — pipeline-stage visibility
Dual SAML Cert Rotation ⚠ Not confirmed in public docs — must be verified ⚠ Not confirmed in public docs — must be verified
Chase accesses mParticle via browser session cookies, not bearer tokens — a security gap below JPMC standards. Both platforms' Terraform providers are available; only mParticle's is not deployed. Dual SAML certificate rotation support must be explicitly confirmed by both vendors.
Where Each Platform Has the Edge

mParticle Advantages

  • IDSync — 5 configurable identity strategies with a granular priority hierarchy. More expressive than Segment's single framework.
  • ComposeID — Warehouse-native identity resolution Chase owns and controls. No Segment equivalent.
  • Cortex — Native ML audience engine (churn, LTV, propensity) included in Enterprise licence. Segment requires an external ML platform.
  • Zero-copy architecture — PII stays in Chase's environment. No new GDPR obligations or third-party PII processing agreements.
  • Data Catalog — Central event registry with definitions and ownership. Included, not deployed.

Segment Advantages

  • Profiles Insights — Identity diagnostic layer showing exactly why profiles did or did not merge. Chase explicitly needs this.
  • S2S catalogue — 400+ integrations vs 300+. Less reliance on on-device kit translation.
  • Egress rate controls — More granular per-destination throttling natively. No custom Lambda required.
  • Amplitude cohort feedback — Amplitude cohorts natively feed Segment audience activation. mParticle rejected this request.
  • Nested JSON — No pre-flattening required before ingestion.
  • Workspace RBAC — More granular read/write/admin isolation including read-only Production access.
Chase Issue Classification — Product Gap vs Deployment Gap
This classification matters: deployment gaps are addressable within the current mParticle contract. Product gaps would exist even after migrating to Segment in most cases.
ISSUE
Classification
Segment Resolves?
Detail
—
Paid media audiences not activated
Deployment
mParticle's paid media connectors are included and functional. Chase has not implemented them. Same configuration work required on Segment.
—
AppsFlyer data discrepancy
Deployment
S2S integration is available on mParticle and would resolve the kit translation issue. Segment's S2S also requires AppsFlyer device_id — same constraint applies.
—
IDSync underutilised (thin identity graph)
Deployment
mPID, IDFV, GAID are natively supported but not configured. Segment would require the same identity configuration work.
—
Terraform not implemented
Deployment
mParticle Terraform provider is available. A deployment gap, not a product gap. Segment's provider is more mature but both are available.
—
DSR failure (CUID-only AppsFlyer requests)
Product/AppsFlyer
Root cause is AppsFlyer's OpenDSR API requiring a device ID. Segment faces the identical limitation. Neither platform resolves this without AppsFlyer changing their API.
—
Egress self-throttle (rejected)
Product Gap
Genuine mParticle product limitation. Segment resolves natively with granular per-destination rate limiting.
—
Amplitude cohort feedback (rejected)
Product Gap
Genuine mParticle product gap (GOC 000182). Segment supports Amplitude as both source and destination natively.
Migration Considerations — If Segment Were Selected
The GOC tracker evidence, four Critical debt items, seven rejected requests and the Cortex/ComposeID deployment gap give Chase strong grounds for renegotiating the mParticle contract. This should be exhausted before migration is scoped.
SDK Re-implementation
All five on-device SDKs would need to be re-implemented. This is a significant engineering effort requiring QA, staged rollout, and ATT consent re-verification on iOS.
Data Plan Migration
All Data Plans and schema governance rules would need to be recreated in Segment's Protocols. No automated migration path exists.
Warehouse Architecture
Switching to Segment introduces data movement from the Data Lake into Segment's infrastructure (Reverse ETL). This creates new GDPR data processing obligations and requires a fresh security review with a banking risk committee.
Zero-copy Loss
The current mParticle zero-copy model — under which PII never leaves Chase's environment — would be lost. This is a material security architecture downgrade for a regulated financial institution.
moEngage Re-integration
The existing mParticle-moEngage S2S integration is working well and powers live campaign operations. It would need to be recreated and validated on Segment before cutover.
Renegotiation Alternative
The documented GOC evidence gives Chase strong leverage: 4 Critical debt items, 7 rejected requests including compliance-impacting ones, and 58% of all requests in permanent backlog. A renegotiation would likely yield faster resolution of Cortex, ComposeID, and egress controls than a full migration.
Summary: On verified product capability, mParticle and Segment are closely matched. mParticle's meaningful advantages — Cortex, ComposeID, zero-copy architecture, 5 identity strategies — align directly with Chase's banking security posture and data governance requirements. The majority of Chase's gaps are deployment decisions addressable within the current contract. The two genuine product gaps (egress throttle, Amplitude cohort feedback) are real but narrow relative to the migration cost and security architecture trade-offs.
Tab 03 / Future State Architecture · Scenario 1

Optimise mParticle —
Reduce SDK Bloat, Fix Data Discrepancy,
Improve Data Governance

This scenario retains mParticle as the core CDP but fundamentally transforms how it operates — from an event router with on-device kit translation, to a fully optimised server-side data backbone with deduplication, schema validation, real-time audience activation and a closed-loop analytics and attribution pipeline. The Enterprise Data Backbone includes Deduplication, Schema Validation & Observability.

Original Architecture Diagram

Scenario 1 — Optimise mParticle · MarTech Future State · Original Diagram

Click diagram to toggle full width

Scenario Overview

What Changes

  • Kit-based AppsFlyer → S2S integration
  • Manual file push for paid media → real-time audience syndication
  • 5 on-device SDKs → mParticle wrapper only
  • Ad-hoc schema validation → enforced Data Master with Dedup Gate
  • No observability → full Live Stream + alerting pipeline

What's Preserved

  • Zero-copy architecture — PII stays in Chase environment
  • Dual Kinesis streams to Data Lake
  • moEngage bidirectional S2S
  • mParticle as single SDK wrapper on-device
  • Consent Manager / Login Intercept gate

What's Unlocked

  • Real-time paid media audience activation
  • Cortex ML propensity / churn / LTV models
  • ComposeID warehouse-native identity
  • Experian third-party enrichment via 3PA
  • Advanced Analytics Engine (LTV, Churn, Propensity)
  • GDPR DSR compliance fix path (via S2S device_id)
Architecture — Layer by Layer
Layer 01 — Data Origin

Chase — All Customer Touchpoints

All customer interactions across digital and offline channels feed into the data pipeline. This is the first and most critical expansion in the future state — offline sources are brought online for the first time.

Digital Channels

Android, iOS, websites (chase.co.uk). Existing SDK coverage. Web events, app events, in-app actions all captured.

Offline Channels (NEW)

Call centre, ATM interactions, Branch visits, In-person sales. Ingested via S2S Events API as batch or near-real-time streams into mParticle S2S Spine.

↓
Layer 02 — Consent Gate

Consent Manager + Login Intercept

No consent = No SDK initialisation. No events captured. No data flows downstream. This gate is preserved in its current form and remains the entry condition for all subsequent data flows. OneTrust / Consent Manager integration governs per-destination consent state, gating data at source rather than filtering post-collection.

Post-consent-only: the on-device mParticle SDK only initialises after explicit consent is granted. This is non-negotiable for Chase's UK compliance posture.
↓
Layer 03 — On-Device (Post-Consent Only)

SDK Consolidation — From 5 SDKs to 1 Wrapper

The current architecture runs 5 on-device SDKs simultaneously (mParticle, AppsFlyer, Amplitude, moEngage, Split). This creates SDK bloat, maintenance overhead, and kit-translation data discrepancies. The future state consolidates to the mParticle wrapper only on-device. All partner SDKs are removed from the device and replaced by S2S integrations.

mParticle SDK (Wrapper)

Single on-device SDK. Handles ATT consent, event collection, identity (mPID), and forwards to mParticle S2S Spine only. No partner kit bundled.

AppsFlyer Kit → S2S

AppsFlyer moves from on-device kit to S2S integration. af_id is now captured as a proper device dimension in mParticle. Resolves the AppsFlyer / Amplitude attribution discrepancy. Step B in the migration.

Step A: Why Kit → S2S Matters

Currently, AF data flows through AF kit transformation → gets dropped → finds its way to Data Lake via a separate path. af_id is never captured as a device dimension in mParticle. This breaks identity and attribution. S2S fixes this at source.

↓
Layer 04 — Core · mParticle S2S Spine

Optimised Enterprise Data Backbone & Data Governance Layer

This is the central transformation in Scenario 1. The mParticle S2S Spine replaces the current fragmented kit-translation architecture with a fully server-side optimised pipeline. All inbound data — from device, from S2S partners, from offline sources — passes through this spine before reaching any downstream destination.

🔵 Deduplication Gate

Input: Source name, message ID, message_type, batching
Output: client_dedupe_id with context
Function: Compresses multiple events matching dedupe criteria into a single event. Deduplicates event streams across Sessions, Users, Devices, and Stores.

🟢 Data Master

Schema Validation & Consent Plan. Data Plan: JSON schema validates event name, attributes, required fields and allowed values. Smart Types, required field enforcement, date-getbuild failures. Rules Engine: enrichment & reconciliation before forwarding. Live Streams: real-time validation indicators. Data Catalog: central registry of all data points.

🔴 Failed Validation Handler

Schema violations = events logged with error type. Review and replay capability — events forwarded to output if schema violation is not-dropped (clean). Dupes caught here are logged, not dropped silently.

S2S Destinations

All AF S2S API, Amplitude HTTPS, mEngage S2S destinations receive clean, validated, deduplicated events with error-type tagging.

Event Hub

Clean, validated, deduplicated events distributed to all downstream platforms simultaneously.

Identity Resolution

Single collection point. IDSync receives all identifier signals (mPID, CUID, af_id, IDFV, GAID). mParticle profiles with all identities merged, cross-device tracking fully activated.

Audience Sync

mParticle Audiences real-time sync to Amplitude, moEngage, and — critically — all paid media platforms. Real-time activation replaces manual file push.

Single Event Sink Rule: Consent = mParticle SDK set → mParticle Servers → S2S to all destinations. No consent = No SDK init → No events captured → Data flows only if conditions are met. AF S2S attribution only when this pipeline is active.
↓
Layer 05 — Observability

Live Stream + Data Plan Monitoring + Unnamed/Unplanned Events

For the first time, Chase has full real-time visibility into the data pipeline. Three parallel observability channels run continuously:

Live Stream

Real-time payload inspection. See every event as it passes through the spine. Debug integration issues immediately rather than waiting for downstream data discrepancies.

Data Plan Monitoring

Schema violation rate, failed validation counts, dropped event tracking. Alerts trigger when violation rate exceeds threshold.

Unnamed / Unplanned Events

New event types that arrive without a matching Data Plan schema are flagged immediately — not silently ingested. Governance is proactive, not reactive.

↓
Layer 06 — Vendor Platforms

All Receive Validated + Deduplicated + Consent-Gated Events

Every downstream vendor platform — Amplitude, moEngage, AppsFlyer (S2S), PopupSmart — receives the same clean, validated, consent-gated, deduplicated event stream. The current state where each platform receives subtly different data due to kit translation is eliminated.

Amplitude (Analytics)

Clean events. Cohort feedback loop now enabled. Feature flags via Split remain active.

moEngage (Engagement)

Bidirectional S2S preserved. Campaign attribution closed-loop continues.

AppsFlyer (Attribution)

Now via S2S. af_id correctly captured. Attribution discrepancy with Amplitude resolved. DSR path now viable.

Data Lake (Source of Truth)

Dual Kinesis streams preserved. Now receives deduplicated, validated event stream — higher quality than current raw feed.

↓
Layer 07 — Data Lake, Enrichment & Activation Loop

Reverse ETL & Postback Loop — Closing the Attribution Circle

The Data Lake becomes a bidirectional participant, not just a destination. Enriched data from the Lake feeds back into the activation pipeline. This layer enables the four core use cases that are currently impossible: LTV/Propensity modelling, enrichment, historical backfill, and sales reconciliation.

Use Case 1: LTV / Propensity

Enriched profiles (mParticle + AppsFlyer attribution + Amplitude analytics) feed Advanced Analytics Engine. Cortex ML models generate LTV scores, churn probability, and propensity signals for each customer.

Use Case 2: Enriched Audiences

Experian third-party data (3PA) ingested via Batch Ingest to Data Lake. Segments and cohorts built on enriched profiles for paid media targeting. Real-time sync to Google Ads, Meta, GMP, Apple Search Ads, Rakuten.

Use Case 3: Historical Backfill

LiveTran sync: AF (sync only, no destination) + Amplitude (Market) + mEngage (Starter) feed historical event data back through the Data Lake for retroactive attribution and cohort analysis.

Use Case 4: Sales Reconciled

Offline sales data (Call centre, ATM, Branch) is reconciled against digital attribution data in the Data Lake, producing a complete cross-channel view of customer acquisition cost and lifetime value.

↓
Layer 08 — Third-Party Audience Data (3PA)

Experian Integration — Enrichment at Scale

Experian data is ingested via Batch Ingest to the Data Lake (S3 / Athena / equivalent). This enrichment layer adds attributes that Chase's first-party data cannot capture alone — demographics, credit segments, life-stage indicators, and proprietary propensity scores.

Data Clean Room

Attribute distribution between Chase PTO (first-party) and Experian 3P data. Ingestion at ad partner level (Google, Meta) combined with a single attribution view. All linkage done in one ecosystem to maintain zero-copy security model.

Paid Partnerships

No-brand card pages, merchant category enrichment, franchise analysis, partner transaction targeting (Foursquare), identity coordination (mailchimp) — all feeding audience segmentation for paid media.

Activation Options

Option 1 (3PA): Enriched profiles in Data Lake → Livetran syncs audiences → mParticle syndicates to Google Ads, Meta, GMP + event-level data to Amplitude, mEngage, Rakuten.
Option 2 (Recommended): Composable Audiences query the warehouse directly (zero-copy) for real-time segment refresh via built-in connectors. Bi-directional to Data Lake.

↓
Layer 09 — Advanced Analytics Engine

Predictive Modelling at Scale

Once the data spine is clean and the enrichment loop is closed, the Advanced Analytics Engine runs continuously on the unified Data Lake to generate forward-looking signals for marketing and product decisions.

LTV Prediction

Predicts modules: LTV, Churn, Propensity, Intent, Visit propensity, Churn (30/60/90d).

Segmentation Engine

Multi-attribute segmentation: 37-LTV + ingestion for model testing. Cohort → Segment real-time audience generation from warehouse compute.

Paid Media Precision

High-value segments (37+ LTV score) surfaced for suppression, look-alike, and prospecting across all ad platforms. Composable Audiences with warehouse-native compute — no data movement.

↓
Layer 10 — Ad Platform Postbacks

Real-Time Activation Across All Paid Channels

The final layer replaces the current manual file push mechanism entirely. All paid media platforms receive clean, real-time audiences and conversion postbacks directly from mParticle Audience Sync — no manual security reviews, no batch delays, no governance risk.

DMP

Programmatic audience seeding. Real-time segment sync.

Google Ads / GMP

Customer Match, Similar Audiences, conversion postbacks. Was: manual file push.

Meta

Custom Audiences via S2S. Real-time suppression and look-alike seeding.

Apple Search Ads

S2S audience sync. SKAN postbacks from AppsFlyer S2S flow preserved.

Rakuten

Affiliate conversion postbacks and audience signals. Previously unsupported.

What Scenario 1 Unlocks vs Current State
Real-Time Paid Media Audiences
Manual file push (days, governance risk) → real-time S2S audience syndication to all 5 ad platforms
Fixed
AppsFlyer Attribution Discrepancy
Kit translation drops af_id → S2S captures af_id as device dimension, resolving Amplitude discrepancy
Fixed
GDPR DSR Forwarding
CUID-only requests fail → S2S ensures device_id is present, making DSR forwarding to AppsFlyer viable
Path Resolved
SDK Bloat (5 → 1)
5 on-device SDKs with kit translation overhead → single mParticle wrapper, all partners via S2S
Fixed
Cortex ML Audiences
Inactive despite being in licence → Churn, LTV, Propensity models active once clean data pipeline established
Unlocked
Experian Enrichment
No third-party data → Experian segments ingested via Batch Ingest, feeding enriched paid media audiences
Unlocked
Offline Data Ingestion
Call centre, Branch, ATM interactions siloed → ingested via S2S Events API, unified into customer profile
Unlocked
Schema & Deduplication Governance
Partial enforcement → full Dedup Gate + Data Master validation on all events before any downstream forwarding
Upgraded
Scenario 1 is additive, not disruptive. It does not require replacing mParticle. It requires activating capabilities that are already licensed, fixing the S2S integration gap, and closing the manual file push workaround. The same security posture, the same zero-copy architecture, and the same vendor relationships are preserved — with the full value of the £1.4M investment finally realised.
